input.title: Walters v. OpenAI — ChatGPT defamation hallucination (appellate question)
input.description: Radio host Mark Walters sued OpenAI after ChatGPT, in response to a journalist's prompt, allegedly fabricated a false claim that Walters had embezzled funds from the Second Amendment Foundation. Trial court granted OpenAI summary judgment on grounds that disclaimers and reasonable-recipient standard precluded actionable defamation. Appeal asks whether AI-hallucinated factual statements with named real persons can constitute publisher fault notwithstanding disclaimers.

events.description: Journalist submits prompt to ChatGPT requesting summary of a Second Amendment Foundation lawsuit
ChatGPT generates response that fabricates allegations of embezzlement attributed to Mark Walters, who is not party to the actual lawsuit
Output contains specific named-person false claim of financial misconduct without any factual basis in training data or prompt
Disclaimer surface relied upon as cure under summary judgment; appellate question whether sufficient to defeat reasonable-recipient standard
Journalist reviewed output and chose not to publish; no harm pleading on actual publication — appellate question whether mere generation is publication

prediction._note: Anchor-log prediction. Produced by the same deterministic scoring engine that runs at faultkey.com/try. Sealed by SHA256 commitment + OpenTimestamps + Sigstore Rekor; revealed when the underlying case resolves.
